subsystem: HHVM (HipHop Virtual Machine) PHP runtime extension implementing the PHP built-in function mcrypt_create_iv(int $size, int $source = MCRYPT_DEV_RANDOM). It generates an initialization vector (IV) of $size bytes for the legacy mcrypt symmetric-encryption extension, drawing entropy either from /dev/random, /dev/urandom, or the C library rand() PRNG, and returns it as an HHVM String (Variant) to PHP userland.
provenance: Identified from the HHVM-specific macro Variant HHVM_FUNCTION(mcrypt_create_iv, ...) which is HHVM's built-in-function registration idiom, the HHVM value types Variant and String(...,...,AttachString), and the raise_warning() diagnostic helper — all part of the HHVM runtime. The function name mcrypt_create_iv and the RANDOM / URANDOM source selectors map directly onto PHP's ext/mcrypt mcrypt_create_iv() API (MCRYPT_RAND / MCRYPT_DEV_RANDOM / MCRYPT_DEV_URANDOM). The leading comment /* sample: primevul_train_2092_vuln */ is a corpus tag and is treated as untrusted, non-authoritative.
trust_boundary: This is a PHP-callable API: both parameters (size and source) are fully attacker/caller-controlled from PHP userland, and the returned IV is used as cryptographic material by whatever encryption the caller performs. The security-critical boundary is the QUALITY of the bytes written into the IV buffer: an IV that an attacker can predict undermines the confidentiality/integrity guarantees of the cipher that consumes it. The source selector decides which entropy path runs, and its default value (0) routes to the weakest path (rand()). The file descriptor content from /dev/(u)random is trusted; the C rand() stream is not a cryptographic source and is effectively predictable to a network/offline attacker.

what_the_code_does.code: Variant HHVM_FUNCTION(mcrypt_create_iv, int size, int source /* = 0 */) {
what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares the HHVM built-in that backs PHP's mcrypt_create_iv(). It takes two ints: size, the number of IV bytes the caller wants, and source, a selector for the entropy source whose default value is 0 (the comment /* = 0 */ documents the default argument). Both are supplied directly from PHP userland, so both are caller-controlled. The return type Variant lets the function return either a PHP false (on error) or a String value (the IV). Because source defaults to 0, a caller that writes simply mcrypt_create_iv($size) selects whatever branch 0 maps to; as seen below, 0 matches neither RANDOM nor URANDOM and therefore falls through to the rand()-based else branch.

Validates the requested IV length. It rejects any size that is not strictly positive and any size at or above INT_MAX, emitting a PHP warning and returning false. This bounds size to the open interval (0, INT_MAX), which guarantees that the later size + 1 on line 10 cannot overflow a signed int (the maximum value size+1 can reach is INT_MAX, which is representable). It is a correct, defensive gate on the length; it does not, however, say anything about the quality of the bytes that will fill the buffer.

n is a running counter used to record how many bytes were actually produced; it is initialized to 0 and later becomes either read_bytes (device path) or size (rand path). iv is a heap buffer of size + 1 bytes obtained via calloc, which zero-fills the allocation — the extra trailing byte provides a NUL terminator slot so the returned String is NUL-safe. calloc(size+1, 1) computes count*element = (size+1)*1 with no multiplication-overflow concern. The return value of calloc is cast straight to char* and used later WITHOUT a NULL check, which is significant on allocation failure.

The strong-entropy branch. Only when the caller explicitly passes source == RANDOM (MCRYPT_DEV_RANDOM) or source == URANDOM (MCRYPT_DEV_URANDOM) does the code open the corresponding kernel entropy device read-only. If open() fails (fd < 0), it frees the IV buffer, warns, and returns false — correct cleanup. Note the entry condition: the default source of 0 does NOT satisfy this test (assuming the MCRYPT_RAND constant is 0), so the default caller never reaches this cryptographically sound path.

Loops reading from the device until size bytes are gathered or an error occurs. Each iteration reads up to the remaining count size - read_bytes into iv + read_bytes and stores the return in n. The loop continues while read_bytes < size and n >= 0 (i.e., no read error). On a read error, read() returns -1, so n becomes -1, the guard n >= 0 fails, and the loop exits. After the loop the code sets n = read_bytes (total bytes actually written) and closes the descriptor. The subtle case is read() returning 0 (end-of-file / no data available without blocking), which leaves n == 0: the loop guard n >= 0 stays true and read_bytes does not advance, so the loop can spin.

After the read loop, this verifies that the full size bytes were actually collected. If fewer bytes were read (partial read, error, or truncation), it frees the buffer, warns, and returns false rather than returning a short/partly-uninitialized IV. This is a correct completeness check for the device path — it prevents handing back an IV that is not fully populated with device entropy.

The fallback branch, taken for every source value that is not RANDOM or URANDOM — including the DEFAULT source of 0. It fills the buffer backwards: while size is nonzero, it writes iv[--size] with a byte derived from the C standard library rand(). The expression 255.0 * rand() / RAND_MAX scales rand()'s output (a value in [0, RAND_MAX]) into the floating range [0.0, 255.0] and truncates to a char. n is set to size up front so the whole buffer is reported as valid. rand() is a deterministic, non-cryptographic PRNG typically seeded from time/process state; the resulting IV bytes are therefore predictable, not secret.

Wraps the raw buffer in an HHVM String and returns it to PHP. AttachString transfers ownership of the malloc'd iv pointer to the String object (the String will free it), and n is the logical length. Because n equals size on both success paths and the buffer is size+1 with a zero terminator at index size, the String is well-formed. The returned bytes are exactly the IV the caller will feed to a cipher.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core cryptographic defect. When source is anything other than RANDOM/URANDOM — and crucially when it is the DEFAULT value 0 (line 2) — the IV is filled from the C rand() PRNG. rand() is not a cryptographically secure generator: its internal state is small, it is usually seeded from low-entropy values (time, PID) that an attacker can guess or brute-force, and its output stream is fully determined by that seed. An attacker who can recover or guess the seed can reconstruct the entire IV. A predictable IV defeats the security of the cipher that consumes it (e.g., enabling chosen-plaintext/known-IV attacks against CBC/CTR modes, or breaking IV-uniqueness assumptions), even when the key stays secret. Because the weak path is the DEFAULT, the most common call form mcrypt_create_iv($size) silently gets predictable IVs. The invariant: an IV intended for cryptographic use MUST come from a CSPRNG / kernel entropy source, never from rand(); ideally the weak source should not be selectable at all, and certainly must not be the default. Secondary, minor artifact: 255.0 * rand() / RAND_MAX yields a non-uniform byte distribution (value 255 occurs only for rand()==RAND_MAX, and floating truncation biases the low bytes), but this is dwarfed by the predictability problem.

The calloc return value is used without a NULL check. On allocation failure (which an attacker can push toward by requesting a very large size — up to nearly INT_MAX bytes, since the only cap is size < INT_MAX on line 3), calloc returns NULL. The code then dereferences iv unconditionally: in the device branch read(fd, iv + read_bytes, ...) writes into NULL+offset, and in the rand branch iv[--size] = ... writes through a NULL pointer. Either is a NULL-pointer dereference / wild write that crashes the HHVM process (denial of service). The invariant: every malloc/calloc/realloc result must be NULL-checked before use, and on failure the function must clean up and return false (as it already does for the open() and short-read failures).

The loop termination depends on read() either filling the buffer or returning a negative error. It does NOT handle read() returning 0. read() returns 0 to signal end-of-file / no more data; for /dev/random this normally does not happen (it blocks instead), but if the descriptor's behavior differs (e.g., the device is exhausted-and-closed semantics on some platforms, a restricted/emulated environment, or a race that yields EOF), n becomes 0, read_bytes stops advancing, and the guard n >= 0 remains true — an infinite loop that hangs the request thread and burns CPU (denial of service). The invariant: a read loop must treat a 0-byte read as a terminal condition (break and report insufficient data), not merely as "non-negative, keep going." The impact is bounded compared with the crypto defect but is a genuine latent hang.

summary: The dominant issue is cryptographic: the default source (0) routes IV generation through the non-cryptographic C rand() PRNG (lines 29-33), producing predictable initialization vectors and undermining any cipher that uses them (CWE-330). Secondary defects are an unchecked calloc return that becomes a NULL-pointer write on large-size allocation failure (CWE-476, line 10) and a read loop that can hang on a 0-byte read (CWE-835, lines 19-21). The size validation and device-path completeness checks are correct.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: HHVM_FUNCTION(mcrypt_create_iv, int size, int source /* = 0 */) — signature and default source=0 (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Default source value 0 does not match RANDOM/URANDOM and therefore routes the common call form to the weak rand() branch.
Caller-controlled length but properly bounded to (0, INT_MAX) at lines 3-7; no overflow or under-allocation results.
Selects the entropy path; its default and any non-device value fall through to the insecure rand() branch.
Correct bounds check; rejects non-positive and too-large sizes and prevents size+1 signed overflow.
Return value dereferenced without a NULL check; allocation failure leads to a NULL-pointer write.
Selector values that correctly gate the /dev/random and /dev/urandom paths; the constants themselves carry no bug.
Proper cryptographic source with correct error cleanup (free + warn + return false).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: read loop for(read_bytes=0; read_bytes<size && n>=0; read_bytes+=n) (lines 19-21)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Does not treat read()==0 as terminal, permitting a potential infinite loop / hang.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: n = read_bytes and if (n < size) short-read check (lines 22-28)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correct completeness check that rejects partially filled IVs from the device path.
Uses a non-cryptographic PRNG to generate cryptographic IV bytes; predictable output.
Ownership transfer of the buffer to the String is correct; n matches the populated length and the buffer is NUL-terminated at index size.

findings.title: Predictable cryptographic IV generated from non-CSPRNG rand() (default path)
findings.anchor: iv[--size] = (char)(255.0 * rand() / RAND_MAX); reached via default source == 0
findings.bug_class: Use of insufficiently random values for a cryptographic IV (predictable PRNG)
findings.invariant: IV bytes handed to a cipher must originate from a cryptographically secure source (kernel entropy / CSPRNG); the C library rand() must never fill cryptographic material, and the weak source must not be reachable by default.
findings.failure_mode: A PHP caller invoking mcrypt_create_iv($size) with the default source (0) — or any source other than MCRYPT_DEV_RANDOM/URANDOM — receives an IV composed of rand() output. rand() is a low-entropy, deterministic PRNG typically seeded from time/PID, so an attacker who guesses or recovers the seed reconstructs the IV, enabling known-IV/chosen-plaintext attacks and breaking IV-uniqueness for modes like CBC/CTR even while the key remains secret.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the numeric value of the MCRYPT_RAND constant that source defaults to (that 0 selects this else branch), and confirm no callers rely on the rand() path; verify the fix routes all sources to /dev/urandom or an equivalent CSPRNG.

findings.title: Unchecked calloc return dereferenced (NULL-pointer write on allocation failure)
findings.bug_class: NULL-pointer dereference via unchecked allocation return
findings.invariant: The result of calloc must be checked for NULL before any dereference; on failure the function must free nothing-yet-allocated appropriately and return false, mirroring the existing open()/short-read error handling.
findings.failure_mode: The caller can request a size just under INT_MAX; if calloc cannot satisfy it, it returns NULL. The code then writes through iv unconditionally — read(fd, iv + read_bytes, ...) at line 20 or iv[--size] = ... at line 32 — dereferencing NULL and crashing the HHVM process (denial of service).
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify there is no NULL guard on iv after line 10 in the shipped implementation and that a large-size request can actually drive calloc to fail on the target platform.

findings.title: Potential infinite loop when read() returns 0
findings.anchor: for (read_bytes = 0; read_bytes < size && n >= 0; read_bytes += n)
findings.bug_class: Loop with unreachable exit condition (infinite loop / hang)
findings.invariant: A read loop must treat a 0-byte read as a terminal condition and break (then report insufficient data), rather than only exiting on a negative return.
findings.failure_mode: If read() returns 0 (EOF / no data), n becomes 0, read_bytes does not advance, and the guard n >= 0 stays true, so the loop spins forever — hanging the request thread and consuming CPU. While /dev/random typically blocks rather than returning 0, an EOF/altered-descriptor condition makes this a real latent DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether read() on the selected device can return 0 in the deployment environment and that no separate guard breaks the loop on a zero-length read.

audit_checklist: Confirm the MCRYPT_RAND/default source value is 0 so mcrypt_create_iv($size) reaches the rand() branch, and eliminate the rand() path (route all sources to a CSPRNG / getrandom / /dev/urandom).
Add a NULL check on the calloc result at line 10 with proper cleanup-and-return-false handling.
Change the device read loop to break on a 0-byte read and only accept the IV when exactly size bytes were gathered.
Verify the returned String length n and buffer ownership (AttachString) match on all paths and that the trailing NUL slot is preserved.
Review all PHP callers for reliance on the insecure default source, and consider removing MCRYPT_RAND as a selectable option.
